feedback.text: Greetings! first of all I have to say that I am grateful to have the opportunity to express myself about this topic, as the world is going more and more into a digital future, I believe I have to address a very important part of the digital world that should be considered for as well, that being the entertainment side, specifically, videogames. Back when the only way to play videogames was by downloading them using floppy disks, CD's, etc. it was considered that the consumer was buying a copy of the game. That led to said consumer being able to use their product as long as they wanted, even after 10 years or more. As time went on, the industry evolved, and so did ways to download videogames on the gaming device, which today means online downloads. That is the "golden standard" of selling videogames, but, it unfortunately also led to companies selling instead of copies, licenses. That means that you, as the consumer, no longer own your product. This loophole led to many beloved games, such as "The Crew", "The Culling 1&2", "Dauntless", and many more, even archived on a wiki website from a movement called "Stop Killing Games", that has reached over 1 million signatures on their petition, to introduce to the consumer, even after the developers pull the plug and decide to move on, an option to be able to still play the game, like he did before. A good example of games that are under risk of such issues would be "MMO's" (Massively Multiplayer Online) games and "Live Service" games, as for the MMO they require active players, and Live Service games require constant updates, as that is their business model. Both of these genres of games usually can get revived using, for example, "private servers" that are community hosted and allow for the experience to keep going. I hope this essay brings to light this problem, and helps with consideration towards fixing it by implementing laws that would not allow publishers to just remove all access towards a game, and allow us to enjoy our media for as long as we want to, even if it involves community work like private servers. Have a wonderful day!
